version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/3] nfsd: follow-on fixes for CB_NOTIFY support

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/3] nfsd: fix CB_NOTIFY workqueue loop when queue overflows
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a workqueue loop issue in the NFS server when the event queue overflows. When the queue is full, the prepare function would jump to the recall path without draining the queue, causing the release operation to requeue the callback indefinitely. The patch drains the queued events and resets the event count before recalling, preventing the requeue and fixing an event reference leak. Additionally, it guards against a revoked delegation on the release side, avoiding unnecessary notifications and pinning of the state ID.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] nfsd: fix NULL deref / UAF of sc_export in setup_notify_fhandle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes two issues in the NFS server code: a NULL dereference and a use-after-free bug. The bug occurs when an admin revokes a delegation, which can cause the export to be cleared and its reference dropped independently of the sc_count reference held by the CB_NOTIFY callback. To fix this, the patch grabs a reference to the export under the client's lock and guards every use of the export with a NULL check. This ensures that the export is not accessed after it has been freed or cleared, preventing both the NULL dereference and use-after-free bugs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/3] nfsd: recall deleg if a requested dir attr change can't be encoded
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 server where it silently drops directory attribute change notifications if they cannot be encoded. The patch recalls the delegation when such a notification is requested but cannot be delivered, as required by RFC 8881. It achieves this by treating a failure to encode the dir attribute change like other per-event encode failures and falling through to recall the delegation.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues pointed out by Sashiko in review, specifically fixing problems related to CB_NOTIFY support in the NFS daemon. The patches fix a workqueue loop issue when the queue overflows, prevent a NULL dereference and use-after-free error in setup_notify_fhandle, and recall delegations if requested directory attribute changes cannot be encoded.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/4] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink
[PATCH v4 0/4] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] sunrpc: add per-netns per-procedure call counts to svc_stat
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-network namespace (netns) and per-procedure call counts to the sunrpc service statistics. It introduces a new array in struct svc_stat to store these counts, which are allocated and freed using helper functions. The existing global call count arrays are not network-namespace-aware, so this change is necessary for an upcoming netlink stats interface that requires namespace-scoped statistics. The patch also updates the nfsd_net_init and nfsd_net_exit functions to manage the new per-netns counts.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the svc_stat_alloc_counts() function is not called in nfsd_net_init() when the net namespace is being initialized, and requested that it be added to ensure per-net statistics are properly allocated.

The reviewer noted that the patch breaks the build for an NFSv3-only server configured with CONFIG_PROC_FS=n due to svc_stat_alloc_counts() and svc_stat_free_counts() being defined in net/sunrpc/stats.c, which is only built under PROC_FS. The declarations in <linux/sunrpc/stats.h> sit outside the #ifdef CONFIG_PROC_FS block without !PROC_FS stubs, leading to an undefined reference to svc_stat_alloc_counts at vmlinux link (NFSD=y) or a modpost failure for NFSD=m.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hey Jeff -

On Tue, Jun 16, 2026, at 8:45 AM, Jeff Layton wrote:

---

This patch appears to break the build for an NFSv3-only server
configured with CONFIG_PROC_FS=n.

svc_stat_alloc_counts() and svc_stat_free_counts() are defined in
net/sunrpc/stats.c, which the Makefile builds only under PROC_FS:

       sunrpc-$(CONFIG_PROC_FS) += stats.o

nfsd_net_init()/nfsd_net_exit() call both helpers unconditionally,
and the declarations in <linux/sunrpc/stats.h> sit outside the
#ifdef CONFIG_PROC_FS block with no !PROC_FS stubs. CONFIG_NFSD does
not depend on PROC_FS -- only NFSD_V4 does -- so NFSD=y with
NFSD_V2/V3 and PROC_FS=n is a valid Kconfig combination. The result
is an undefined reference to svc_stat_alloc_counts at vmlinux link
(NFSD=y), or a modpost failure for NFSD=m since both symbols are
EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL.

net/sunrpc/svc.c is always built, and might be a more natural home
for the new functions.

-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] sunrpc: use per-net counts in svc_seq_show()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the svc_seq_show() function to read procedure call counts from per-network-namespace arrays instead of global counters. This change allows /proc/net/rpc/nfsd to report procedure call counts for each network namespace separately. The patch only affects the nfsd service, which always allocates these counters via a specific initialization function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] nfsd: implement server-stats-get netlink handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a netlink handler to expose NFS server statistics, making it possible for tools like nfsstat to retrieve these stats via the generic netlink family. The handler streams statistics across multiple messages, first sending scalar stats and then per-operation counts in subsequent messages. This allows older kernels without /proc/net/rpc/nfsd support to still access server statistics through a procfs fallback.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the server-proc-entry attribute set is missing a 'count' attribute to track the number of operations for each procedure

Reviewer noted that the netlink dump handler is missing a counter for wdeleg_getattr, which is present in the procfs output but not emitted by the dump handler. The reviewer suggested adding this counter to the server-stats attribute-set in nfsd.yaml and emitting it as a single scalar in the start message.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hey Jeff -

On Tue, Jun 16, 2026, at 8:45 AM, Jeff Layton wrote:

---

The procfs output in nfsd_show() emits one more counter that this dump
handler drops: wdeleg_getattr (NFSD_STATS_WDELEG_GETATTR), printed at
the end of the CONFIG_NFSD_V4 block in fs/nfsd/stats.c, right after
proc4ops. The netlink dump stops after proc4ops-ops and sets
cb->args[0] = -1, so the write-delegation GETATTR-conflict counter never
goes out on the wire.

There's no schema slot for it either: the server-stats attribute-set
ends at proc4ops-ops, so a consumer that prefers netlink and only falls
back to procfs on an old kernel loses this counter on every kernel new
enough to support server-stats-get.

I Suggest:

- add a wdeleg-getattr (u64) attribute to the server-stats
  attribute-set in nfsd.yaml and list it under the dump reply
  attributes;

- add NFSD_A_SERVER_STATS_WDELEG_GETATTR to the uapi enum, after
  PROC4OPS_OPS and before __NFSD_A_SERVER_STATS_MAX so existing
  values don't shift;

- emit it as a single scalar in the start == 0 message, wrapped in
  CONFIG_NFSD_V4 to match the procfs side.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about the usefulness of adding a new stat field and explained that it was not currently accessed by any userland tool, but left open the possibility of adding it later if needed.
review_comments.raw_body:
This omission was intentional. The stated purpose of this patchset was
to convert nfsstat to use netlink. nfsstat does not access or display
this field today. Without something that consumes this stat, I don't
see the point of adding it here. We can always add it later when/if it
becomes useful to userland.

Is there something that accesses this today that I'm not aware of? If
not, is there some future intent to add this to nfsstat?
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the new netlink API should match the existing procfs API to prevent a regression and suggested mentioning the excluded feature in the commit message.

reviewer noted that the 'wdeleg-getattr' field was missing from nfsstat's display, and requested its inclusion
review_comments.raw_body:
If we were adding a greenfield kernel-userspace API, I would agree.
However, this API is meant to be compatible with the existing
procfs API, so it should match to prevent a regression (I'm not
aware of a consumer, this is only a general guideline).

For future reference: since a missing feature cannot be expressed
in a patch's diff body, your intention to exclude this field could
be mentioned in the commit message.

---

I think nfsstat should display the wdeleg-getattr field. It has simply
been forgotten.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agreed to add a new attribute to the netlink interface in v5 and acknowledged that nfs-utils will need a follow-on patch to display the value.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok. I'll plan to add it to the interface and send it in v5. nfs-utils
will need a follow-on patch to display the value. No promises on when
I'll do that, but it shouldn't be too hard.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is addressing a concern about how to handle wdeleg_getattr in nfsstat, suggesting an alternative approach of exposing server-side callback call counts instead of keeping the current statistic.
review_comments.raw_body:
I have a drafted patch, but I'm not thrilled with it. wdeleg_getattr is
weird in that it's not a statistic about server-side protocol
operations. So, it ends up sort of sitting off on its own in nfsstat.

What would you (and Dai) think about instead keeping and exposing
server-side callback call counts? Then we could just add a new section
to nfsstat server-side output with all of the callback operation counts
in it. wdeleg_getattr would just be the count for CB_GETATTRs.

Thoughts?
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] sunrpc: remove unused svc_version vs_count field
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused vs_count field from struct svc_version and its associated percpu counters across various NFS-related modules, including lockd and nfsd. The removal is justified by the fact that svc_seq_show() and the nfsd netlink stats handler now use the per-netns svc_stat vs_count arrays instead of the global per-version counters. This change simplifies the code and reduces memory usage.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink instead of /proc/net/rpc/nfsd. The new interface provides per-network-namespace procedure call counts and exposes them through a new netlink handler in the nfsd generic netlink family. This allows nfsstat to retrieve server statistics via netlink with a procfs fallback for older kernels. The patches add per-netns counters, switch svc_seq_show() to read from these counters, implement the server-stats-get netlink dump handler, and remove unused global arrays.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a netlink dump handler fails to send all requested data, and suggested adding error handling for this scenario.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v7 00/20] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 00/20] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations
[PATCH v7 00/20] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 01/20] nfsd: check fl_lmops in nfsd_breaker_owns_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a check to the nfsd_breaker_owns_lease() function to verify that the lease being checked is an NFS daemon-managed lease by checking if its fl_lmops field matches the nfsd_lease_mng_ops structure. This ensures that only leases created by the NFS daemon are considered for ownership checks, preventing potential issues with other types of leases.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 02/20] nfsd: add protocol support for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds protocol support for CB_NOTIFY in the nfsd module, which is part of the Linux kernel's Network File System (NFS) implementation. It introduces changes to various header files and regenerates XDR (External Data Representation) files to accommodate the new feature. The patch also removes duplicate definitions from nfs4.h and uapi nfs4 header, and updates constants such as NFS4_VERIFIER_SIZE and NFS4_FHSIZE according to RFC requirements. Additionally, it modifies the nfsstat4 enum to ensure that internal error codes do not leak onto the wire.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 03/20] nfs_common: add new NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new NOTIFY4_* flags to the NFS protocol, as proposed in RFC8881bis. These flags allow Linux nfsd to omit information about directory cookies and ordering when providing directory delegations. The changes are based on a working group document that has been stable for over a year, but there is still some risk of future changes. The patch updates several files, including the NFS protocol documentation, the xdr generator code, and the header file for the NFS protocol.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 04/20] nfsd: allow nfsd to get a dir lease with an ignore mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the NFS server to obtain a directory lease with an ignore mask, enabling it to selectively ignore certain events such as file creation, deletion, and renaming. This is achieved by modifying the nfsd_get_dir_deleg function to pass the requested notification types to the nfs4_alloc_init_lease function, which sets the corresponding flags in the file lease. The patch also introduces a new function, nfsd_notify_to_ignore, that maps notification types to their corresponding ignore mask bits.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 05/20] nfsd: update the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a directory delegation in the NFS server. It adds a new helper function, nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask(), which recalculates the fsnotify mask based on the leases present. The function is called when directory delegations are added or removed and ensures that concurrent updates to the lease state do not clobber each other's changes. This patch also introduces a mutex to serialize access to the recalculation of the mark, preventing stale snapshots from being used in the calculation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 06/20] nfsd: make nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation bool return
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation to return a boolean value indicating whether processing should continue or not. The operation is used in various callback scenarios, such as CB_NOTIFY operations, where allocation failures need to be handled by stopping further processing. The changes affect several files and functions within the NFS server code, ensuring that the prepare operation returns true if processing can continue and false otherwise.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 07/20] nfsd: add callback encoding and decoding linkages for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds callback encoding and decoding linkages for the CB_NOTIFY operation in the NFSv4 protocol. It introduces new functions to encode and decode CB_NOTIFY messages, which will be used by the NFS server to handle client notifications. The encoder is currently a stub, but later patches will flesh out the payload encoding. This change is part of a larger series of patches that aim to improve the NFSv4 implementation in the Linux kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 08/20] nfsd: use RCU to protect fi_deleg_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch uses RCU (Read-Copy Update) to protect the fi_deleg_file pointer in the nfsd kernel module. The problem it solves is a race condition that can occur when multiple threads access the fi_deleg_file pointer concurrently, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. To fix this, the patch annotates the fi_deleg_file pointer with __rcu and converts all accessors to use RCU primitives. This prepares for a subsequent patch that will safely acquire a reference from the CB_NOTIFY callback path without holding the fi_lock lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 09/20] nfsd: add data structures for handling CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds data structures and functionality to support directory delegation CB_NOTIFY in the NFSv4 protocol. It introduces two new structs, nfsd_notify_event and nfsd4_cb_notify, which carry fsnotify events and per-delegation state for notification handling, respectively. The patch also refactors alloc_init_deleg into a common function with a pluggable sc_free callback and adds alloc_init_dir_deleg to allocate necessary resources for CB_NOTIFY encoding. Additionally, it sets up skeleton nfsd4_cb_notify_ops with done/release handlers that will be filled in when the notification path is wired up.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 10/20] nfsd: add notification handlers for dir events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for directory change events in the NFS server (nfsd). It introduces notification handlers to handle dir events, allocates a nfsd_notify_event object, and hands off references to each delegation's CB_NOTIFY. If any failures occur during this process, it recalls affected delegations. The changes are implemented across multiple files, including filecache.c, nfs4callback.c, nfs4state.c, and others.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 11/20] nfsd: apply the notify mask to the delegation when requested
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the NFS server to apply a notify mask to directory delegations when requested by clients. The notify mask is set based on the client's request, taking into account whether they require notifications for directory offset information or ordering. If the client doesn't request notifications or requires them but can't be supported by the Linux server, the notify mask is zeroed out. The granted notification mask is also stored in the delegation's dl_notify_mask field to allow later encoding of notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 12/20] nfsd: add helper to marshal a fattr4 from completed args
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a helper function to marshal the attribute values of an NFSv4 file attribute (fattr4) from completed arguments. The new function, nfsd4_encode_attr_vals(), breaks out the encoding of attr_vals into its own separate function, improving code organization and maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 13/20] nfsd: allow nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() to work with no export
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() function to work even when there is no export available, which can occur in certain situations such as a CB_NOTIFY callback. The change ensures that the function behaves correctly and safely in these cases by adding a check for the presence of an export before attempting to access it. This patch addresses a specific edge case where nfsd would not grant a delegation on the V4 root, making this modification necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 14/20] nfsd: send basic file attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to send basic inode information in a CB_NOTIFY event, including attributes that can be acquired via GETATTR. The patch modifies the nfsd4_setup_notify_entry4 function to include inode attributes and encode them in the xdr stream. This allows clients to receive updated file attributes when notified of changes to files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 15/20] nfsd: allow encoding a filehandle into fattr4 without a svc_fh
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the encoding of a filehandle into fattr4 without requiring a svc_fh. It achieves this by adding a new 'fhandle' field to struct nfsd4_fattr_args and populating it from the svc_fh in CB_NOTIFY callbacks. The MAC append function is generalized to operate on bare knfsd_fh, and the FSID attribute shares the same attrmask gate as the filehandle.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 16/20] nfsd: add the filehandle to returned attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new routine to compose a filehandle for the CB_NOTIFY callback in NFSd, which simplifies the process by not requiring a svc_export and its associated machinery. The new routine uses the parent filehandle from the nfs4_file and the child dentry to fill out the fhandle field in the nfsd4_fattr_args structure. This change is necessary because the existing fh_compose routine was too complex for the CB_NOTIFY callback's needs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 17/20] nfsd: fix reply size estimate for GET_DIR_DELEGATION
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue in the NFS server where the estimated reply size for the GET_DIR_DELEGATION operation was too small, leading to a failure to encode the reply and leaving the client without the returned stateid. The fix is to multiply the estimate by the size of a __be32 word, like other _rsize helpers. This ensures that the reply will fit within the session/reply limit.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 18/20] nfsd: properly track requested child attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves how the NFS server handles child attributes in directory delegations. It correctly tracks the union of requested and supported child attributes, ensuring that only the necessary attributes are encoded when sending add/remove/rename updates. The patch also adjusts the budget for encoding child attribute bitmaps to account for the increased size of the bitmap when word1 attributes are included.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 19/20] nfsd: track requested dir attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the NFS daemon (nfsd) to track the union of requested and supported directory attributes in the delegation. This change is necessary because the requested dir attributes can now include word1 attributes, which may result in a two-word bitmap being emitted by nfsd4_encode_bitmap4(). To account for this larger encoding, the dir-attribute bitmap budget in nfsd4_get_dir_delegation_rsize() is bumped from one word to two. This patch lays the groundwork for ensuring that only the necessary directory attributes are encoded when sending add/remove/rename updates.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 20/20] nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support to the NFS server (nfsd) to send directory attribute change notifications to clients using the CB_NOTIFY operation. When a client requests dir attribute change notifications, the server will send these notifications alongside other events such as add, remove, and rename operations. The server still recalls the delegation on a SETATTR operation, so these notifications are only sent for changes to child directories. The patch modifies several files in the nfsd codebase to implement this new functionality.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patchset adds support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations, allowing clients to register interest in a directory and receive notifications about changes within it without losing its lease. The series includes patches to allow the vfs to ignore certain types of events on directories, set up inotify watches on the directory, and send updated directory attributes in notifications. The patchset has been tested with pynfs and new testcases are being posted soon. The goal is to enable clients to receive notifications about creates, unlinks, and renames within a directory, improving performance and reducing lease loss issues.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about lease ownership checks in nfsd_breaker_owns_lease(), agreeing to add a quick check for the fl_lmops being set to nfsd_lease_mng_ops before testing whether the breaker owns the lease.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about duplicate definitions in the nfs4_1.x file and removed them, also regenerating the xdr files to resolve conflicts with constants defined by the RFC.
review_comments.raw_body:
Add the necessary bits to nfs4_1.x and remove the duplicate definitions
from nfs4.h and the uapi nfs4 header. Regenerate the xdr files.

Note that regenerating these files caused conflicts with the definitions
of NFS4_VERIFIER_SIZE and NFS4_FHSIZE in include/uapi/linux/nfs4.h.
These constants are defined by the RFC, and are not part of the kernel
API. They have been removed. Userspace consumers who require those
constants should plan to get them from more authoritative sources.

The nfsstat4 enum defined in the .x is fed to the xdrgen-generated wire
encoder and decoder, which treat every enumerated value as legal on the
wire. Do not carry the NFS4ERR_FIRST_FREE sentinel (which is not a
protocol error code) into the .x; keeping it would make 10097 a value
that could leak onto the wire. Instead base nfsd's internal error codes
(NFSERR_EOF and friends) at an impossible nfsstat4 value, as lockd does
for its nlm__int__* status codes.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

 include/linux/sunrpc/xdrgen/nfs4_1.h | 279 ++++++++++++++++-
 include/uapi/linux/nfs4.h            |   2 -
 8 files changed, 1105 insertions(+), 145 deletions(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/sunrpc/xdr/nfs4_1.x b/Documentation/sunrpc/xdr/nfs4_1.x
index 5b45547b2ebc..72d439b71735 100644
--- a/Documentation/sunrpc/xdr/nfs4_1.x
+++ b/Documentation/sunrpc/xdr/nfs4_1.x
@@ -45,19 +45,162 @@ pragma header nfs4;
 /*
  * Basic typedefs for RFC 1832 data type definitions
  */
-typedef hyper		int64_t;
-typedef unsigned int	uint32_t;
+typedef int                  int32_t;
+typedef unsigned int         uint32_t;
+typedef hyper                int64_t;
+typedef unsigned hyper       uint64_t;
+
+const NFS4_VERIFIER_SIZE        = 8;
+const NFS4_FHSIZE               = 128;
+
+enum nfsstat4 {
+ NFS4_OK                = 0,    /* everything is okay      */
+ NFS4ERR_PERM           = 1,    /* caller not privileged   */
+ NFS4ERR_NOENT          = 2,    /* no such file/directory  */
+ NFS4ERR_IO             = 5,    /* hard I/O error          */
+ NFS4ERR_NXIO           = 6,    /* no such device          */
+ NFS4ERR_ACCESS         = 13,   /* access denied           */
+ NFS4ERR_EXIST          = 17,   /* file already exists     */
+ NFS4ERR_XDEV           = 18,   /* different filesystems   */
+
+ /*
+  * Please do not allocate value 19; it was used in NFSv3
+  * and we do not want a value in NFSv3 to have a different
+  * meaning in NFSv4.x.
+  */
+
+ NFS4ERR_NOTDIR         = 20,   /* should be a directory   */
+ NFS4ERR_ISDIR          = 21,   /* should not be directory */
+ NFS4ERR_INVAL          = 22,   /* invalid argument        */
+ NFS4ERR_FBIG           = 27,   /* file exceeds server max */
+ NFS4ERR_NOSPC          = 28,   /* no space on filesystem  */
+ NFS4ERR_ROFS           = 30,   /* read-only filesystem    */
+ NFS4ERR_MLINK          = 31,   /* too many hard links     */
+ NFS4ERR_NAMETOOLONG    = 63,   /* name exceeds server max */
+ NFS4ERR_NOTEMPTY       = 66,   /* directory not empty     */
+ NFS4ERR_DQUOT          = 69,   /* hard quota limit reached*/
+ NFS4ERR_STALE          = 70,   /* file no longer exists   */
+ NFS4ERR_BADHANDLE      = 10001,/* Illegal filehandle      */
+ NFS4ERR_BAD_COOKIE     = 10003,/* READDIR cookie is stale */
+ NFS4ERR_NOTSUPP        = 10004,/* operation not supported */
+ NFS4ERR_TOOSMALL       = 10005,/* response limit exceeded */
+ NFS4ERR_SERVERFAULT    = 10006,/* undefined server error  */
+ NFS4ERR_BADTYPE        = 10007,/* type invalid for CREATE */
+ NFS4ERR_DELAY          = 10008,/* file "busy" - retry     */
+ NFS4ERR_SAME           = 10009,/* nverify says attrs same */
+ NFS4ERR_DENIED         = 10010,/* lock unavailable        */
+ NFS4ERR_EXPIRED        = 10011,/* lock lease expired      */
+ NFS4ERR_LOCKED         = 10012,/* I/O failed due to lock  */
+ NFS4ERR_GRACE          = 10013,/* in grace period         */
+ NFS4ERR_FHEXPIRED      = 10014,/* filehandle expired      */
+ NFS4ERR_SHARE_DENIED   = 10015,/* share reserve denied    */
+ NFS4ERR_WRONGSEC       = 10016,/* wrong security flavor   */
+ NFS4ERR_CLID_INUSE     = 10017,/* clientid in use         */
+
+ /* NFS4ERR_RESOURCE is not a valid error in NFSv4.1 */
+ NFS4ERR_RESOURCE       = 10018,/* resource exhaustion     */
+
+ NFS4ERR_MOVED          = 10019,/* filesystem relocated    */
+ NFS4ERR_NOFILEHANDLE   = 10020,/* current FH is not set   */
+ NFS4ERR_MINOR_VERS_MISMATCH= 10021,/* minor vers not supp */
+ NFS4ERR_STALE_CLIENTID = 10022,/* server has rebooted     */
+ NFS4ERR_STALE_STATEID  = 10023,/* server has rebooted     */
+ NFS4ERR_OLD_STATEID    = 10024,/* state is out of sync    */
+ NFS4ERR_BAD_STATEID    = 10025,/* incorrect stateid       */
+ NFS4ERR_BAD_SEQID      = 10026,/* request is out of seq.  */
+ NFS4ERR_NOT_SAME       = 10027,/* verify - attrs not same */
+ NFS4ERR_LOCK_RANGE     = 10028,/* overlapping lock range  */
+ NFS4ERR_SYMLINK        = 10029,/* should be file/directory*/
+ NFS4ERR_RESTOREFH      = 10030,/* no saved filehandle     */
+ NFS4ERR_LEASE_MOVED    = 10031,/* some filesystem moved   */
+ NFS4ERR_ATTRNOTSUPP    = 10032,/* recommended attr not sup*/
+ NFS4ERR_NO_GRACE       = 10033,/* reclaim outside of grace*/
+ NFS4ERR_RECLAIM_BAD    = 10034,/* reclaim error at server */
+ NFS4ERR_RECLAIM_CONFLICT= 10035,/* conflict on reclaim    */
+ NFS4ERR_BADXDR         = 10036,/* XDR decode failed       */
+ NFS4ERR_LOCKS_HELD     = 10037,/* file locks held at CLOSE*/
+ NFS4ERR_OPENMODE       = 10038,/* conflict in OPEN and I/O*/
+ NFS4ERR_BADOWNER       = 10039,/* owner translation bad   */
+ NFS4ERR_BADCHAR        = 10040,/* utf-8 char not supported*/
+ NFS4ERR_BADNAME        = 10041,/* name not supported      */
+ NFS4ERR_BAD_RANGE      = 10042,/* lock range not supported*/
+ NFS4ERR_LOCK_NOTSUPP   = 10043,/* no atomic up/downgrade  */
+ NFS4ERR_OP_ILLEGAL     = 10044,/* undefined operation     */
+ NFS4ERR_DEADLOCK       = 10045,/* file locking deadlock   */
+ NFS4ERR_FILE_OPEN      = 10046,/* open file blocks op.    */
+ NFS4ERR_ADMIN_REVOKED  = 10047,/* lockowner state revoked */
+ NFS4ERR_CB_PATH_DOWN   = 10048,/* callback path down      */
+
+ /* NFSv4.1 errors start here. */
+
+ NFS4ERR_BADIOMODE      = 10049,
+ NFS4ERR_BADLAYOUT      = 10050,
+ NFS4ERR_BAD_SESSION_DIGEST = 10051,
+ NFS4ERR_BADSESSION     = 10052,
+ NFS4ERR_BADSLOT        = 10053,
+ NFS4ERR_COMPLETE_ALREADY = 10054,
+ NFS4ERR_CONN_NOT_BOUND_TO_SESSION = 10055,
+ NFS4ERR_DELEG_ALREADY_WANTED = 10056,
+ NFS4ERR_BACK_CHAN_BUSY = 10057,/*backchan reqs outstanding*/
+ NFS4ERR_LAYOUTTRYLATER = 10058,
+ NFS4ERR_LAYOUTUNAVAILABLE = 10059,
+ NFS4ERR_NOMATCHING_LAYOUT = 10060,
+ NFS4ERR_RECALLCONFLICT = 10061,
+ NFS4ERR_UNKNOWN_LAYOUTTYPE = 10062,
+ NFS4ERR_SEQ_MISORDERED = 10063,/* unexpected seq.ID in req*/
+ NFS4ERR_SEQUENCE_POS   = 10064,/* [CB_]SEQ. op not 1st op */
+ NFS4ERR_REQ_TOO_BIG    = 10065,/* request too big         */
+ NFS4ERR_REP_TOO_BIG    = 10066,/* reply too big           */
+ NFS4ERR_REP_TOO_BIG_TO_CACHE =10067,/* rep. not all cached*/
+ NFS4ERR_RETRY_UNCACHED_REP =10068,/* retry & rep. uncached*/
+ NFS4ERR_UNSAFE_COMPOUND =10069,/* retry/recovery too hard */
+ NFS4ERR_TOO_MANY_OPS   = 10070,/*too many ops in [CB_]COMP*/
+ NFS4ERR_OP_NOT_IN_SESSION =10071,/* op needs [CB_]SEQ. op */
+ NFS4ERR_HASH_ALG_UNSUPP = 10072, /* hash alg. not supp.   */
+                                /* Error 10073 is unused.  */
+ NFS4ERR_CLIENTID_BUSY  = 10074,/* clientid has state      */
+ NFS4ERR_PNFS_IO_HOLE   = 10075,/* IO to _SPARSE file hole */
+ NFS4ERR_SEQ_FALSE_RETRY= 10076,/* Retry != original req.  */
+ NFS4ERR_BAD_HIGH_SLOT  = 10077,/* req has bad highest_slot*/
+ NFS4ERR_DEADSESSION    = 10078,/*new req sent to dead sess*/
+ NFS4ERR_ENCR_ALG_UNSUPP= 10079,/* encr alg. not supp.     */
+ NFS4ERR_PNFS_NO_LAYOUT = 10080,/* I/O without a layout    */
+ NFS4ERR_NOT_ONLY_OP    = 10081,/* addl ops not allowed    */
+ NFS4ERR_WRONG_CRED     = 10082,/* op done by wrong cred   */
+ NFS4ERR_WRONG_TYPE     = 10083,/* op on wrong type object */
+ NFS4ERR_DIRDELEG_UNAVAIL=10084,/* delegation not avail.   */
+ NFS4ERR_REJECT_DELEG   = 10085,/* cb rejected delegation  */
+ NFS4ERR_RETURNCONFLICT = 10086,/* layout get before return*/
+ NFS4ERR_DELEG_REVOKED  = 10087, /* deleg./layout revoked   */
+ NFS4ERR_PARTNER_NOTSUPP = 10088,
+ NFS4ERR_PARTNER_NO_AUTH = 10089,
+ NFS4ERR_UNION_NOTSUPP = 10090,
+ NFS4ERR_OFFLOAD_DENIED = 10091,
+ NFS4ERR_WRONG_LFS = 10092,
+ NFS4ERR_BADLABEL = 10093,
+ NFS4ERR_OFFLOAD_NO_REQS = 10094,
+ NFS4ERR_NOXATTR = 10095,
+ NFS4ERR_XATTR2BIG = 10096
+};
 
 /*
  * Basic data types
  */
+typedef opaque		attrlist4<>;
 typedef uint32_t	bitmap4<>;
+typedef opaque		verifier4[NFS4_VERIFIER_SIZE];
+typedef uint64_t        nfs_cookie4;
+typedef opaque		nfs_fh4<NFS4_FHSIZE>;
 
 typedef opaque		utf8string<>;
 typedef utf8string	utf8str_cis;
 typedef utf8string	utf8str_cs;
 typedef utf8string	utf8str_mixed;
 
+typedef utf8str_cs      component4;
+typedef utf8str_cs      linktext4;
+typedef component4      pathname4<>;
+
 /*
  * Timeval
  */
@@ -66,6 +209,21 @@ struct nfstime4 {
 	uint32_t	nseconds;
 };
 
+/*
+ * File attribute container
+ */
+struct fattr4 {
+        bitmap4         attrmask;
+        attrlist4       attr_vals;
+};
+
+/*
+ * Stateid
+ */
+struct stateid4 {
+        uint32_t        seqid;
+        opaque          other[12];
+};
 
 /*
  * The following content was extracted from draft-ietf-nfsv4-delstid
@@ -245,3 +403,82 @@ const FATTR4_ACL_TRUEFORM	= 89;
 const FATTR4_ACL_TRUEFORM_SCOPE	= 90;
 const FATTR4_POSIX_DEFAULT_ACL	= 91;
 const FATTR4_POSIX_ACCESS_ACL	= 92;
+
+/*
+ * Directory notification types.
+ */
+enum notify_type4 {
+        NOTIFY4_CHANGE_CHILD_ATTRS = 0,
+        NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS = 1,
+        NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY = 2,
+        NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY = 3,
+        NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY = 4,
+        NOTIFY4_CHANGE_COOKIE_VERIFIER = 5
+};
+
+/* Changed entry information.  */
+struct notify_entry4 {
+        component4      ne_file;
+        fattr4          ne_attrs;
+};
+
+/* Previous entry information */
+struct prev_entry4 {
+        notify_entry4   pe_prev_entry;
+        /* what READDIR returned for this entry */
+        nfs_cookie4     pe_prev_entry_cookie;
+};
+
+struct notify_remove4 {
+        notify_entry4   nrm_old_entry;
+        nfs_cookie4     nrm_old_entry_cookie;
+};
+
+struct notify_add4 {
+        /*
+         * Information on object
+         * possibly renamed over.
+         */
+        notify_remove4      nad_old_entry<1>;
+        notify_entry4       nad_new_entry;
+        /* what READDIR would have returned for this entry */
+        nfs_cookie4         nad_new_entry_cookie<1>;
+        prev_entry4         nad_prev_entry<1>;
+        bool                nad_last_entry;
+};
+
+struct notify_attr4 {
+        notify_entry4   na_changed_entry;
+};
+
+struct notify_rename4 {
+        notify_remove4  nrn_old_entry;
+        notify_add4     nrn_new_entry;
+};
+
+struct notify_verifier4 {
+        verifier4       nv_old_cookieverf;
+        verifier4       nv_new_cookieverf;
+};
+
+/*
+ * Objects of type notify_<>4 and
+ * notify_device_<>4 are encoded in this.
+ */
+typedef opaque notifylist4<>;
+
+struct notify4 {
+        /* composed from notify_type4 or notify_deviceid_type4 */
+        bitmap4         notify_mask;
+        notifylist4     notify_vals;
+};
+
+struct CB_NOTIFY4args {
+        stateid4    cna_stateid;
+        nfs_fh4     cna_fh;
+        notify4     cna_changes<>;
+};
+
+struct CB_NOTIFY4res {
+        nfsstat4    cnr_status;
+};

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the stability of RFC8881bis, which adds new flags to GET_DIR_DELEGATION that later patches will consume. The author explained that while RFC8881bis is still a working group document and has been for years, the changes to directory delegations have been stable for the last year or so, and hopes that those parts won't change (much).
review_comments.raw_body:
RFC8881bis adds some new flags to GET_DIR_DELEGATION that later patches
will consume. In particular, Linux nfsd can't easily provide info about
directory cookies and ordering. The new flags allow it to omit that
information.

There is some risk here -- RFC8881bis is still a working group document,
and has been for years. The changes to directory delegations have been
stable for the last year or so however, so the hope is that those parts
won't change (much).

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about enabling the correct notification types for directory leases, explaining that the FL_IGN_DIR_* bits should be set based on the requested notification types. The author added a new function nfsd_notify_to_ignore() to calculate the corresponding flags and updated nfs4_alloc_init_lease() to use this function.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 01/10] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching and sending the next request in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver from io_uring, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. The duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() is removed, and a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() is introduced to fetch the next request without sending it immediately. This change simplifies the code and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 02/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which simplifies the code and centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging. The changes affect several functions in fs/fuse/dev_uring.c, making it easier to maintain and modify the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 03/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts this logic into a new function called copy_header_from_ring(), making it clearer and more centralized, and also improves error handling and logging.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 04/10] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE file system by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This simplifies both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying, particularly when buffer rings are added. The patch introduces an enum type `fuse_uring_header_type` with values for different header types and modifies existing functions to use this new type. The changes improve code readability and maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 05/10] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver, specifically for payload copying. A new helper function called `setup_fuse_copy_state` is introduced to encapsulate this logic, making the code more modular and easier to maintain. The function takes several parameters, including a pointer to the copy state structure, a fuse ring, a request, an entry in the ring, and an iterator. It initializes the copy state and imports user buffers as needed. This patch is part of a larger series of changes to improve the FUSE driver's performance and reliability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 06/10] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices 0 and 1 in the iovec array with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support. The changes are made in the fuse:dev_uring.c file, specifically in the functions fuse_uring_get_iovec_from_sqe and fuse_uring_create_ring_ent. This patch is part of a larger series of 10 patches and has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass in a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used as needed by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. A buffer ring data structure is used to manage the buffer pool, selecting and recycling buffers on demand.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about queue setup and configuration, proposing to add a new command operation (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) to pass in queue-level settings, including the number of reduced queues, buffer pool, and flags. This would decouple queue creation from sqe registration and provide a clean way to set up queue configurations.
review_comments.raw_body:
Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
(FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
like a reasonable tradeoff.

Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about how the server would access specific buffers within the buffer pool, explaining that the server knows the starting address and size of each buffer and can compute the address from the buf_id.

The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about how to return the selected buffer in fuse_uring_ent_in_out, proposing to use a buf_id instead of the buffer address and discussing potential future implications.

Author agrees with Miklos Szeredi's suggestion, confirms they will add uAPI documentation and update the directory.
review_comments.raw_body:
The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.

---

This would have to be returned as part of the struct
fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
use. Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.

---

That sounds good. I'll write some uAPI documentation for this and add
it to that new directory.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about adding support for multiple buffer pools, explaining that a 'uint16_t pool_idx' would be needed in struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out to indicate which pool, and userspace would need to keep track of the starting address of each pool.

The author addressed a concern about how FIXED ops through zero-copy handle buffer indexing, explaining that userspace determines and passes the index to the kernel in the init setup. The author confirmed that no buf index needs to be passed back by the kernel for these operations.

Author acknowledged that the 12-byte limit for buffer pool configuration is due to a uapi definition and agreed to consider increasing it

Author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about supporting multiple buffer pools by proposing the use of a 'pool_idx' field to indicate which pool to use.

Author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about how to manage buffer pools, proposing that the kernel pass back a 'pool index' and let userspace keep track of the registered buffer id.
review_comments.raw_body:
If we later wanted to add support for multiple buffer pools, we would
need to extend the struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out to include a "uint16_t
pool_idx" to indicate which pool. Userspace would be responsible for
keeping track of the starting address of each pool.

---

For FIXED ops not through zero-copy, there's one index for the entire
pool (eg this is the index where the pool resides). It passes this by
setting the sqe->buf_index.
For FIXED ops through zero-copy, userspace is required to register a
sparse buffer where the kernel should place the application's/page
cache's pages. Userspace determines the index into this sparse buffer
for the sqe and it passes this index to the kernel in the init setup
in the "uint16_t zero_copy_index;" field when it registers the sqe.

Neither require the kernel to pass back a buf index corresponding to
the fixed buffer index. For both cases, userspace knows the index for
the fixed buffer (it is the one who determined it) and it is the one
who passes this info to the kernel at setup time.

---

The 12 byte limit comes from the "struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out"
definition in the uapi [1].

---

For multiple buffer pools, I was thinking that would be through a
separate "uint16_t pool_idx" field to indicate which pool.

---

I think we should just pass back "pool index" and have userspace keep
track of the registered buffer id that corresponds to that pool index.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.1/source/include/uapi/linux/fuse.h#L1255

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 08/10] fuse: support registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring for the fuse file system. It allows servers to use an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool, eliminating the overhead of pinning and unpinning user pages per I/O request. The server must register the payload buffers through io-uring and set specific flags during registration. This patch also includes kernel support for registered buffers advertised via a single umbrella flag. Benchmarks show a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads, 4% for direct sequential reads, 8% for buffered randreads, and 6% for buffered sequential reads.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi questioned the addition of a new parameter init_flags to the fuse_uring_bufpool_setup function without providing a specific reason or context.
review_comments.raw_body:
Is there any particular reason for this?  Not saying this is bad, just
wondering...

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about pinned pages being accounted for and limited by the kernel io-uring infrastructure, confirming that this is indeed handled.

Author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern that registered buffers should be supported without the buffer pool feature, explaining that it would add complexity and reduce test surface area. Author believes servers wanting registered buffers also want buffer pools, so no wanted use case is being left out.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, pinned pages are accounted and limited by the kernel io-uring
infrastructure (in io_account_mem() in io_uring/rsrc.c) when userspace
registers the buffers in io-uring.

---

There's no technical reason it can't but it keeps things simpler (with
the buffer pool, userspace can more optimally register the bufferpool
into one fixed slot since the buffers are contiguous), reduces the
surface area of configurations that need to be tested, and allows the
feature to be advertised under the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL capability flag
during init instead of needing a separate flag. I think in practice,
servers that would want the benefits from registered buffers would
also want the benefits from buffer pools, so I don't think we're
leaving out a wanted use case.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zero-copy operations over io-uring in the fuse file system. It eliminates memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server by allowing the server to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The patch introduces a new feature that requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS capabilities, which enables the server to register relevant pages into its io-uring registered buffer table. This allows for more efficient read/write operations by reducing memory copies. The patch also includes documentation of the request flow for zero-copy write path.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)

review_comments.raw_body:
The userspace FIXED_READ call (eg set up usually through the liburing
api io_uring_prep_read_fixed() [1]) takes a buf_index, not an address.
Userspace would pass the ent->zero_copy_index as the buf index.
ent->zero_copy_index corresponds to the fixed buffer where userspace
told the kernel (at sqe registration time through the "uint16_t
zero_copy_index" init param) to place the zero-copied pages for that

Thanks,

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the fixed operations in io-uring require both a buffer index and an address to determine the offset within the registered buffer, which is not currently provided.

Reviewer noted that passing a NULL address to the fixed op in io-uring operations for zero-copy buffers results in an offset of zero, and questioned whether this is correct behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
AFAICS buf_index is required, but is not sufficient.  The fixed ops
require an address as well (to figure out the offset within the
registered buffer).

---

Okay so there are two buffers per sqe, one which is corresponds to a
real userspace address, and a zero copy one that is (by definition)
kernel-only and doesn't actually have an address.

Passing a NULL address to the fixed op seems okay in this case, but
that means the offset within the buffer is always zero.

Or am I misunderstanding something?

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the calculation of the offset into the fixed buffer for zero-copied registered buffers, explaining that io-uring internally sets the buffer's uaddr as 0 and the liburing API computes the offset by subtracting imu->ubuf from the provided address. The author did not indicate any plans to change their code.
review_comments.raw_body:
For zero-copied registered buffers, the "address" you pass in is the
raw offset into the buffer (eg if you want to read into the
zero-copied buffer starting at offset 4k in the buffer, you would call
io_uring_prep_read_fixed(sqe, fd, (void *)4096, len, file_off,
buf_index);).

On the kernel side, fuse uring calls io_buffer_register_bvec() to
place the application pages or page cache pages into the slot for the
registered buffer [1]. io-uring internally sets that buffer's uaddr as
0 (imu->ubuf = 0 in io_kernel_buffer_init() [2]) since the pages are
supplied kernel-side. The liburing io_uring_prep_read_fixed() api will
call into the kernel io_read_fixed() -> io_init_rw_fixed() ->
io_import_reg_buf() -> io_import_fixed(). In io_import_fixed(), the
offset into the fixed buffer is computed with "offset = buf_addr -
imu->ubuf" [3] where buf_addr is the u64 representation of the "void
*addr" that was passed into io_uring_prep_read_fixed().

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260612184840.4058966-4-joannelkoong@gmail.com/
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260612184840.4058966-3-joannelkoong@gmail.com/
[3] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.1/source/io_uring/rsrc.c#L1072

Okay.  Could we have a variant of the _fixed API that takes an offset

This is a liburing API [1]. We could add a variant of the _fixed API
for this (will need to come up with a good name for it...). Internally

For plain (non-zero-copied) registered buffers, the fuse server would
still need to pass in the starting address of the buffer pool, so I
think it would just use the regular io_uring_prep_read_fixed() API and

Why?  The kernel couldn't care less about the userspace address at

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 10/10] docs: fuse: add io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy techniques. The new documentation explains how buffer pools decouple the relationship between entries and payload buffers, allowing for more efficient memory usage and enabling optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. It also describes how to use this feature by setting a flag during registration and providing the payload buffer pool through an iovec payload in the first submission queue element (sqe). The documentation includes a high-level overview of how fuse manages the buffer pool internally, including how buffers are selected, copied, and recycled.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer pools and zero-copy to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing servers to pass in a managed buffer pool that reduces memory usage requirements and enables future optimizations. The decoupling of ents and buffers also eliminates the need for dedicated payload buffers, reducing memory waste. Additionally, the series introduces zero-copy functionality, which allows direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and registered buffers.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author simplified the logic for fetching and sending off the next request, removing duplicated code from fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring(), and decoupling request fetching from the send operation.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the tight coupling between ents and buffers in fuse io-uring, explaining that they have decoupled them by allowing servers to pass in a buffer pool (a contiguous allocation of buffers) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. Buffer pool usage is set on a per-ring basis, and the kernel manages buffer selection and recycling through a buffer ring data structure.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the complexity of using registered buffers by explaining that they require setting an init flag and following standard protocol for using registered buffers, which is advertised to userspace via the FUSE_HAS_IO_URING_BUFPOOL init flag. The author provided benchmark results showing around a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads and a 35% improvement for zero-copy.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the complexity of the zero-copy implementation, explaining that it is necessary to handle both page-backed and non-page-backed arguments in a single request flow. The author provided detailed diagrams of the request flow for both write and read paths, highlighting how pages are registered and unregistered using io_buffer_register_bvec() and io_buffer_unregister(). The author also clarified that some requests may have both page-backed and non-page-backed arguments, which are handled separately.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's feedback about the buffer pool interface being inconsistent and suggested a new command op (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) to pass in queue-level settings, including the number of reduced queues and bufpool. The author also explained that returning the buffer address directly would require 8 bytes, but using buf_id takes up only 2 bytes and allows for easier incremental buffer consumption in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 5:06 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > Allow servers to use an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool.
> > When the server registers buffers with io-uring, the pages backing the
> > buffers are pinned upfront. This eliminates the overhead of
> > pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o
> > request. This also allows servers to use the same registered buffers for
> > subsequent backing store I/O (eg read_fixed/write_fixed), keeping data
> > in the same pinned pages without additional pinning or mapping overhead
> > required.
>
> Are pinned pages accounted and limited?

Yes, pinned pages are accounted and limited by the kernel io-uring
infrastructure (in io_account_mem() in io_uring/rsrc.c) when userspace
registers the buffers in io-uring.

>
> > Registered buffers require the buffer pool (FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL) to be
> > enabled.
>
> Is there any particular reason for this?  Not saying this is bad, just
> wondering...

There's no technical reason it can't but it keeps things simpler (with
the buffer pool, userspace can more optimally register the bufferpool
into one fixed slot since the buffers are contiguous), reduces the
surface area of configurations that need to be tested, and allows the
feature to be advertised under the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL capability flag
during init instead of needing a separate flag. I think in practice,
servers that would want the benefits from registered buffers would
also want the benefits from buffer pools, so I don't think we're
leaving out a wanted use case.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> > registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> > payload in the first sqe.
>
> I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
> the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?

Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
(FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
like a reasonable tradeoff.

Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260529-reduced-nr-ring-queues_3-v5-0-1dc08c2fccf6@bsbernd.com/T/#u

>

> Miklos

>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > Buffer ring request flow
> > ------------------------
> > |  Kernel                                  |  FUSE daemon
> > |                                          |
> > |  [client request arrives]                |
> > |  >fuse_uring_send()                      |
> > |    [select payload buf from pool]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_select_buffer()           |
> > |    [copy headers to ent]                 |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring()                |
> > |    [copy payload to selected buf]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()            |
> > |    [set buf_id in ent_in_out header]     |
> > |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                  |
> > |                                          |  [CQE received]
> > |                                          |  [read headers]
> > |                                          |  [read payload from buf_id]
>
> How does buf_id map to a buffer address?

The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.

>
> Wouldn't it be simpler to provide the buffer address directly?

This would have to be returned as part of the struct
fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
use. Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.

>
> We also need some uAPI documentation (generally not just for this
> patchset).  What about a new directory:
> Documentation/filesystems/fuse/uapi?

That sounds good. I'll write some uAPI documentation for this and add
it to that new directory.

Thanks,
Joanne

> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
>
> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>
> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>
> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>
> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
> registered buffers. Zero copy (patch 9) has a dependency on io-uring registered
> bvec changes in [1].

Overall I think this series is good, the only part notably lacking is
concise uAPI documentation.

I'd even suggest dropping all internal implementation details from the
documentation that was provided, since adding those details to the
commit messages (which you did) should be sufficient.

Thanks,
Miklos


>
> This series is on top of commit f21c8b1a3b740 in for-next.
>
> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
> from eliminating the memcpy.
>
> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
>
> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see
> enough of a clear improvement for writes due to write latency being I/O
> dominated.
>
> The benchmarks were run using:
>   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
>   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
>
> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
> submitted upstream.
>
> Thanks,
> Joanne

> up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
> (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
> number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
> nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of

any fixed number of queues and queue entries is almost never the right one.

prepare the kernel, but final routing update needs to be dynamic.


Thanks,
Bernd

This is a liburing API [1]. We could add a variant of the _fixed API
for this (will need to come up with a good name for it...). Internally
it would still have to set sqe->addr = offset, as in [2], but I think
this is a good idea to make it less confusing.

>
> That would work much better for the plain registered buffers too, as
> the fuse server wouldn't have to look up the address belonging to a
> particular buf_index, just supply the offset that was returned in cqe
> (as you suggested) to the _fixed op.

For plain (non-zero-copied) registered buffers, the fuse server would
still need to pass in the starting address of the buffer pool, so I
think it would just use the regular io_uring_prep_read_fixed() API and
pass in starting_address + offset. It's only for buffers that are
zero-copied (eg pages supplied by the kernel) that the addr is the
offset; for non-zero-copied buffers, the addr must be an actual uaddr.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://github.com/axboe/liburing/blob/650d8fb7acb5952a12530b617f09da75b95c979c/src/include/liburing.h#L673
